subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will become a confident expert on two-dimensional shapes – the flat shapes that live all around you. A 2D shape is any shape that is flat, with only length and width and no thickness, like a picture drawn on paper. You will meet polygons, learn to sort triangles and quadrilaterals by their sides and angles, discover the two kinds of symmetry, and explore how shapes fit together in tessellations and repeating patterns.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify and name polygons by their number of sides, and tell regular from irregular polygons.
Describe a shape using the correct language of sides, vertices (corners) and angles.
Classify triangles by their sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and by their angles (right, acute, obtuse).
Classify quadrilaterals (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite) by their properties.
Find all the lines of symmetry in a 2D shape and understand line (reflective) symmetry.
Understand rotational symmetry and state the order of rotational symmetry of a shape.
Explain what a tessellation is and why some shapes tessellate while others leave gaps.
Recognise, describe and continue repeating geometric patterns.

concepts.title: Polygons and the Language of Shape
concepts.explanation: A polygon is a flat, closed shape made only of straight sides. The word comes from two old Greek words: *poly* meaning ‘many’ and *gon* meaning ‘angle’, so a polygon is literally a ‘many-angled’ shape. To be a polygon, a shape must obey three rules: it must be closed (the sides join all the way round with no gaps), it must have only straight sides (no curves), and the sides must not cross over each other. This means a circle is not a polygon, because its edge is curved. A shape with a gap in its outline is not a…
concepts.example: We name polygons by counting their sides. Learning these names is like learning the names of a big family – once you know them, you can talk about any flat shape with confidence:

concepts.title: Classifying Triangles by Their Sides
concepts.explanation: The triangle is the simplest polygon of all, and one of the most important shapes in the world. It has 3 sides, 3 vertices and 3 angles. Triangles appear everywhere in building and engineering because they are extremely strong and rigid – push on a triangle and it holds its shape, which is why you see triangular braces in roofs, bridges, cranes and market-stall frames. There are two completely different ways to sort triangles: by their sides and by their angles. We will learn both, because a full description often…
concepts.example: Equilateral triangle – all three sides are equal, and all three angles are equal too (each is exactly 60°). This is the *regular* triangle.

concepts.title: Classifying Triangles by Their Angles
concepts.explanation: Using angles, there are three types of triangle: Right-angled triangle – has one angle that is exactly 90° (a right angle). You see these in set squares and in the corner of a roof brace.
concepts.example: Acute triangle – all three angles are less than 90°. Every corner is a small, sharp angle.

concepts.title: Classifying Quadrilaterals
concepts.explanation: A quadrilateral is any polygon with 4 sides, 4 vertices and 4 angles. The word breaks into *quad* (four) and *lateral* (sides). Just as with triangles, there is a whole family of special quadrilaterals, each with its own properties. Another vital fact: the four angles inside any quadrilateral always add up to 360° – a full turn. Here are the important members of the family: 4 lines of symmetry; the regular quadrilateral

concepts.title: Line (Reflective) Symmetry
concepts.explanation: Symmetry is one of the most beautiful ideas in mathematics, and you can see it in flowers, in butterflies, in the patterns on a woven mat, and in the careful designs on a masjid door. A shape has line symmetry (also called reflective or mirror symmetry) when you can fold it along a straight line so that both halves match exactly, with one half sitting perfectly on top of the other. That fold line is called a line of symmetry. The best way to test for a line of symmetry is the fold test: imagine folding the shape…
concepts.example: Let us count the lines of symmetry for shapes you now know well:

concepts.title: Rotational Symmetry
concepts.explanation: There is a second kind of symmetry that has nothing to do with folding. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same as you turn it around its centre point, before it has gone all the way round. Imagine pinning a shape at its centre and slowly spinning it: if at some point during a full turn it looks identical to how it started, it has rotational symmetry. We measure rotational symmetry by its order – the number of times the shape looks the same during one complete turn (one full 360° rotation). To…
concepts.example: A square has rotational symmetry of order 4 – it looks the same after turning a quarter-turn (90°), a half-turn (180°), a three-quarter turn (270°) and a full turn.

methods.title: How to classify a triangle
methods.example: A flat, closed shape has 6 straight, equal sides and 6 equal angles. Name it and say whether it is regular.
steps: Look at the sides: all equal = equilateral; two equal = isosceles; none equal = scalene.
Look at the angles: one 90° = right-angled; all under 90° = acute; one over 90° = obtuse.
Combine both words for a full description, for example ‘acute isosceles’.

methods.title: How to find lines of symmetry
methods.example: A triangle has sides 4 cm, 4 cm and 4 cm. Describe it fully.
steps: Imagine folding the shape along a line.
Check whether both halves land exactly on top of each other.
Count every fold line that works – that is the number of lines of symmetry.

methods.title: How to test for tessellation
methods.example: Two angles of a triangle are 90° and 55°. Find the third angle and classify the triangle by its angles.
steps: Find the angle of the shape that meets at each point.
Check whether copies of that angle can add up to exactly 360°.
If they can, the shape tessellates; if they leave a gap or overlap, it does not.

methods.title: Name the quadrilateral
methods.example: A shape has exactly one pair of parallel sides. What is it?
steps: Solution: Exactly one pair of parallel sides means it is a trapezium.
Check the name the quadrilateral result carefully.
Check the name the quadrilateral result carefully.

methods.title: Count lines of symmetry
methods.example: How many lines of symmetry does an isosceles triangle have, and where is it?
steps: Solution: An isosceles triangle has 1 line of symmetry.
It runs from the top vertex (between the two equal sides) straight down to the middle of the base, so the two equal sides fold onto each other.
Check the count lines of symmetry result carefully.

methods.title: State the order of rotational symmetry
methods.example: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle that is not a square?
steps: Solution: Turn it around its centre.
It looks the same after a half-turn (180°) and after a full turn (360°) – two matching positions.
So the order is 2.
Worked Example 7: Will it tessellate?

workedExamples.title: Name the polygon
workedExamples.prompt: A flat, closed shape has 6 straight, equal sides and 6 equal angles. Name it and say whether it is regular.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Six sides means it is a hexagon. Because all sides are equal AND all angles are equal, it is a regular hexagon.

workedExamples.title: Classify by sides and angles
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle has sides 4 cm, 4 cm and 4 cm. Describe it fully.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: All three sides are equal, so by sides it is equilateral. In an equilateral triangle every angle is 60°, all acute, so by angles it is acute. Full description: an acute equilateral triangle (the regular triangle).

workedExamples.title: Use the 180° rule
workedExamples.prompt: Two angles of a triangle are 90° and 55°. Find the third angle and classify the triangle by its angles.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 90 + 55 = 145. Then 180 − 145 = 35°. Because one angle is exactly 90°, it is a right-angled triangle.

workedExamples.title: Name the quadrilateral
workedExamples.prompt: A shape has exactly one pair of parallel sides. What is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Exactly one pair of parallel sides means it is a trapezium.

workedExamples.title: Count lines of symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does an isosceles triangle have, and where is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: An isosceles triangle has 1 line of symmetry. It runs from the top vertex (between the two equal sides) straight down to the middle of the base, so the two equal sides fold onto each other.

workedExamples.title: State the order of rotational symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle that is not a square?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Turn it around its centre. It looks the same after a half-turn (180°) and after a full turn (360°) – two matching positions. So the order is 2. Worked Example 7: Will it tessellate? Will regular hexagons tessellate? Show your reasoning with angles. Solution: Each angle of a regular hexagon is 120°. Three hexagons meet at a point: 3 × 120° = 360°, a full turn with no gap and no overlap. So yes, regular hexagons tessellate – just like a honeycomb.

workedExamples.title: Will it tessellate?
workedExamples.prompt: What is the order of rotational symmetry of a rectangle that is not a square?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Turn it around its centre. It looks the same after a half-turn (180°) and after a full turn (360°) – two matching positions. So the order is 2. Worked Example 7: Will it tessellate? Will regular hexagons tessellate? Show your reasoning with angles. Solution: Each angle of a regular hexagon is 120°. Three hexagons meet at a point: 3 × 120° = 360°, a full turn with no gap and no overlap. So yes, regular hexagons tessellate – just like a honeycomb.

workedExamples.title: Continue a pattern
workedExamples.prompt: A pattern goes: square, square, circle, square, square, circle … What is the 10th shape?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The unit of repeat is ‘square, square, circle’ – a group of 3. The circles land at positions 3, 6, 9, 12 … The 10th shape is one after the 9th circle, so it is the first item of the next repeat: a square.

workedExamples.title: State the order of rotational symmetry of
workedExamples.prompt: State the order of rotational symmetry of: a) a square b) an equilateral triangle c) a rectangle (not a square).
workedExamples.solution: = 45°; angles are 90°, 45°, 45°, so it is a right-angled isosceles triangle.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Explain whether equilateral triangles tessellate, using the angle that meets at each point.
workedExamples.solution: a) square, b) trapezium, c) kite.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: In the pattern ●▲▲●▲▲●▲▲, what is the unit of repeat and what is the 9th shape?
workedExamples.solution: a) order 4, b) order 3, c) order 2.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A triangle is both isosceles and right-angled. State all three of its angles and explain your reasoning.
workedExamples.solution: 1) The right angle is 90°; the other two must be equal (isosceles) and share the remaining 90°, so each is 45°. Angles: 90°, 45°, 45°.

activities.title: Shape Hunt
activities.materials: just your eyes, and paper to record what you find.
steps: Look around your home, the masjid, the market and your neighbourhood.
Find one example each of: a triangle, a square, a rectangle, a hexagon and a circle.
Sketch each one and label the shape name, the number of sides and the number of vertices.
For each triangle you find, decide whether it is equilateral, isosceles or scalene.
Keep your shape record – you can add to it all through this unit.

activities.title: Build Triangles with Sticks
activities.materials: straws, sticks, or dry stalks, cut to different lengths.
steps: Cut three sticks all the same length and build an equilateral triangle.
Cut two sticks the same and one different, and build an isosceles triangle.
Cut three sticks all different, and build a scalene triangle.
Push gently on each triangle. Notice how a triangle keeps its shape – this is why triangles are used in strong buildings.

activities.title: Paper-Folding Symmetry
steps: Fold a sheet of paper in half, then cut a shape along the folded edge.
Unfold it – the crease is a line of symmetry, and both halves match.
Try folding a square in four ways (two straight, two diagonal) to find all 4 lines of symmetry.
Now try a rectangle: which folds match and which do not? Explain why the diagonal folds fail.

activities.title: Make a Tessellation
steps: Cut out many identical squares, then many identical equilateral triangles.
Fit the squares together on a page with no gaps – you have made a tessellation.
Do the same with the triangles. How many meet at each point?
Now try to tessellate regular pentagons (cut several out). What goes wrong? Explain using the angles.

activities.title: Symmetry in Letters and Names (Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, a small mirror if you have one.
steps: Write the capital letters A, B, C, D, H, M, O, T, X, Y in large print.
Use the fold test (or the mirror) to find which letters have a line of symmetry, and how many.
Now write your own name in capitals and find the symmetry in each letter.
Which letters have rotational symmetry as well? (Hint: try H, O, X, S.)

activities.title: Design a Repeating Pattern (Family or Group Task)
activities.materials: coloured pencils or beads, and paper.
steps: Choose a unit of repeat using shapes and colours, for example ‘triangle-red, circle-blue’.
Repeat your unit across a border strip at least 12 items long.
Ask a family member to predict the 15th and 20th items using your rule.
Swap patterns with someone else and continue each other's designs.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand symmetry or tessellation best? What was the trickiest part? Which shapes surprised you? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Angle Sum Rules The three angles inside any triangle add up to 180 degrees. The four angles inside any quadrilateral add up to 360 degrees. To find a missing angle, add the ones you know and subtract from 180 (triangle) or 360 (quadrilateral).

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Symmetry Rules Line symmetry: fold along the line and both halves match exactly. Rotational symmetry order: how many times a shape looks the same in one full turn. Turn angle = 360 divided by the order of rotational symmetry.

rules.title: Tessellation Rule
rules.text: Shapes tessellate when the angles meeting at each point add up to exactly 360 degrees. Every triangle and every quadrilateral tessellates. Regular pentagons do not.

terms: A closed 2D shape with straight sides
A corner / corners of a shape
All sides and angles equal / not all equal
A fold line where both halves match
Looking the same while turning around a centre
Shapes fitting with no gaps and no overlaps
commonMistakes: Saying a rectangle has 4 lines of symmetry
A rectangle has only 2; a square has 4

realProblems.prompt: Hassan builds a kite from two sticks. The finished shape has two pairs of equal sides next to each other. Name the shape and say how many lines of symmetry it has.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija tiles a wall in Mombasa and wants no gaps. She has square tiles and regular pentagon tiles. Which will work, and why?
realProblems.answer: Square tiles work (four meet at 360°); regular pentagons do not (108° does not divide into 360°).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Square tiles work (four meet at 360°); regular pentagons do not (108° does not divide into 360°).

realProblems.prompt: A triangular road sign near Kismaayo has all three angles equal. What is each angle, and what type of triangle is the sign?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra weaves a border repeating ‘light, dark, dark, dark’. What colour is the 16th bead, and how do you know?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Ibrahim folds a rectangular sheet of paper to test its symmetry. How many fold lines make both halves match, and why do the diagonal folds fail?
realProblems.answer: The repeat ‘light, dark, dark, dark’ has 4 beads; 16 ÷ 4 = 4 exact repeats, so the 16th bead is the last of a repeat, dark.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A triangle is both isosceles and right-angled. State all three of its angles and explain your reasoning.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) The right angle is 90°; the other two must be equal (isosceles) and share the remaining 90°, so each is 45°. Angles: 90°, 45°, 45°.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, using angles, why regular hexagons tessellate but regular pentagons do not.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Regular hexagons have 120° angles and three meet at a point (3 × 120° = 360°), so they tessellate; regular pentagons have 108° angles, which does not divide into 360° (three give 324°, four give 432°), so they leave gaps or overlap.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A shape has rotational symmetry of order 2 but no lines of symmetry. Name a quadrilateral that fits this description.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A parallelogram (that is not a rectangle or rhombus).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In a repeating pattern the unit of repeat has 4 shapes. Which position numbers up to 20 land on the last shape of a repeat?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: With 4 shapes per repeat, the last shape lands on the multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Line (Reflective) Symmetry.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Symmetry is one of the most beautiful ideas in mathematics, and you can see it in flowers, in butterflies, in the patterns on a woven mat, and in the careful designs on a masjid door. A shape has line symmetry (also called reflective or mirror symmetry) when you can fold it along a straight line so that both halves match exactly, with one half sitting perfectly on top of the other. That fold line is called a line of symmetry. The best way to test for a line of symmetry is the fold test: imagine folding the shape…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Rotational Symmetry.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: There is a second kind of symmetry that has nothing to do with folding. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same as you turn it around its centre point, before it has gone all the way round. Imagine pinning a shape at its centre and slowly spinning it: if at some point during a full turn it looks identical to how it started, it has rotational symmetry. We measure rotational symmetry by its order – the number of times the shape looks the same during one complete turn (one full 360° rotation). To…

selfAssessment: I can identify and name polygons by their number of sides, and tell regular from irregular polygons.
I can describe a shape using the correct language of sides, vertices (corners) and angles.
I can classify triangles by their sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and by their angles (right, acute, obtuse).
I can classify quadrilaterals (square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite) by their properties.
I can find all the lines of symmetry in a 2D shape and understand line (reflective) symmetry.
I can understand rotational symmetry and state the order of rotational symmetry of a shape.
I can explain what a tessellation is and why some shapes tessellate while others leave gaps.
I can recognise, describe and continue repeating geometric patterns.
